title: Product BOM Decomposition: Türwächter IoT — Elementary & Secondary Schools

sections.title: NAICS 611110 | Output Type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)
Routing Check
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS** | 611110 — Elementary and Secondary Schools |
| **Sector** | Educational Services (61) — service output |
| **BOM applies?** | **YES** — BOM is of the *product sold into this market* (Türwächter IoT), not of the school. Physical device with full compositional structure. |
| **Output type** | Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard — IoT variant (Art. 990140) |
| **Applicable variant** | Exit Control 179 Pre-alarm + Radio (battery-operated, surface-mount, 868 MHz EasyWave) |

---

sections.title: B.1 — L4 Subsystems
sections.content:
Four subsystems apply. No control/interface subsystem is separate — wireless communication is embedded in the IoT module alongside the primary functional core.

| ID | Subsystem (L4) | Pattern category | School-context relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | **Mechanical Guard Subsystem** | Primary functional core | Blocks door handle; single-motion release per EN 179. Non-negotiable under C2 (power-independent). |
| S2 | **Acoustic Alarm Subsystem** | Primary functional (secondary) | 95 dB alert on activation. Mechanical trigger — zero power required. Fire code mandated. |
| S3 | **Wireless Communication Subsystem** | Control / interface | IoT monitoring layer. Key value driver for facility managers. C6 (protocol lock-in) is primary school risk. |
| S4 | **Power Supply Subsystem** | Auxiliary / support | 9V battery. C4 (battery life) elevated in schools — high door-use frequency in corridors and gym exits. |
| S5 | **Enclosure & Mounting Subsystem** | Containment / packaging | Surface-mounted housing. Vandal-resistance concern in schools (C5 surface-mount limitation). |

---

sections.title: B.3 — Alternatives at Every Level
sections.content:
### S1 — Mechanical Guard Subsystem

#### L3: Key Override Mechanism

| Alternative | Share | Trend | School relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Profile half-cylinder (EN 1303) | 55% | Stable | Standard; familiar to facilities staff |
| Cam lock (tubular) | 25% | Stable | Lower cost; less secure |
| Electronic / RFID key override | 15% | Growing | Audit trail; preferred in newer school access programs |
| No override (manual reset only) | 5% | Declining | Eliminates bypass risk; operationally impractical |

**Confidence:** LOW | **Source:** Analyst estimate; no published split for door alarm override mechanisms

#### L1: Guard Arm / Lever Body Material → L0 Polymer

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| ABS (injection-molded) | 40% | Stable | Cost-effective; good impact absorption; indoor standard |
| PC/ABS blend | 35% | Growing | Flame-retardant grades (UL94 V-0); stronger; preferred where IK vandal ratings matter |
| Zinc alloy die-cast | 15% | Stable | Higher strength; heavier; weather-resistant |
| Aluminium alloy | 10% | Declining | Premium; high cost; rarely justified at this product level |

**Confidence:** MEDIUM | **Source:** PC/ABS market data (Credence Research 2024); Polycase material comparison [polycase.com]

---

### S2 — Acoustic Alarm Subsystem

#### L3: Sound Generation Module — Transducer Type

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Piezoelectric (active piezo element) | ~62% | Stable | Dominant. Low power draw; self-contained oscillator; operates without mains. |
| Electromagnetic buzzer | ~28% | Declining | Coil-based; louder at low frequencies; higher current draw — less compatible with battery-primary constraint C2. |
| Speaker + class-D amplifier | ~10% | Growing | Enables configurable tones/voice alerts; significantly higher power; rare in battery-primary devices. |

**Confidence:** MEDIUM | **Source:** Straits Research Buzzer Market 2024; Cognitive Market Research Electromagnetic Buzzers 2024 [cognitivemarketresearch.com]. Piezo at ≥62% corroborated by two independent sources.

**School-market note:** Piezo is the only option compatible with C2 (mechanical alarm at 0 V). Electromagnetic and speaker variants require continuous power for full volume.

---

### S3 — Wireless Communication Subsystem

#### L3: RF Transmitter Module — Wireless Protocol

This is the most strategically significant design choice for the schools market. C6 (EasyWave protocol lock-in) directly constrains integration with school BMS and access control systems.

| Alternative | Share | Trend | School fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Proprietary sub-1GHz (433/868 MHz, e.g. EasyWave) | ~45% | Declining | Dominant in legacy alarm systems; no native BMS interop; GfS BUS gateway required. |
| BLE 5.x (Bluetooth Mesh) | ~22% | Growing | Short range (~30m); native iOS/Android app; no hub required for small deployments. Good for single-school use. |
| Zigbee / Thread | ~15% | Stable | Mesh topology; used in building automation; requires coordinator hub. |
| LoRaWAN | ~10% | Growing | Long range (4–6 floors through concrete); growing in campus deployments; requires LoRaWAN gateway. Best fit for large school campuses. |
| Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n) | ~5% | Stable | Easy IT integration; high power draw; battery life concern (C4). |
| NB-IoT / LTE-M | ~3% | Emerging | Carrier-grade reliability; subscription cost per device; limited to high-security or national chains. |

**Confidence:** LOW | **Source:** Analyst estimate. LoRaWAN market context: Fortune Business Insights 2025 ($7.7B market). No published protocol-split for door alarm devices specifically.

**School-market note:** LoRaWAN is the best architectural fit for multi-building school campuses (penetrates 4–6 concrete floors). BLE is lowest-friction for single-building retrofits. Current EasyWave protocol creates long-term vendor lock-in risk with school district IT procurement policies.

---

### S4 — Power Supply Subsystem

#### L1: 9V Block Battery — Electrochemical Technology (L0)

High-traffic school corridors elevate battery replacement frequency. C4 (battery life floor) is a primary operational concern for school facilities directors.

| Alternative | Share (IoT door devices) | Trend | Battery life estimate (high-use door) | School fit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Alkaline (e.g. Duracell MN1604) | ~52% | Stable | 12–18 months at moderate use | Universally available; low upfront cost; shorter life under high frequency |
| Lithium primary (e.g. Energizer L522) | ~31% | Growing | 24–36 months at same use | 2–3× life; higher upfront; best choice for hard-to-access doors and gym exits |
| NiMH rechargeable (9V) | ~12% | Stable | Requires recharge every 6–12 months | Reduces waste; operationally intensive; not recommended for unattended locations |
| Proprietary rechargeable pack | ~5% | Emerging | Manufacturer-managed; USB-C or wireless charge | Emerging in premium IoT devices; not available in current GfS product line |

**Confidence:** MEDIUM | **Source:** Mordor Intelligence Primary Battery Market 2025; IoT Battery Market report (Straits Research 2024). 47% of industrial IoT sensors use lithium primary for extended life.

**School-market note:** Lithium primary is strongly recommended for school deployments. At 9–35 mA draw (GfS spec), alkaline 9V cells (~550 mAh) exhaust faster under high door-event frequency (gym exits, main corridor exits during class changes). Lithium 9V cells offer ~1,200 mAh — more than 2× the life.

---

### S5 — Enclosure & Mounting Subsystem

#### L0: Housing Shell Polymer

| Alternative | Share (security device housings) | Trend | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| ABS (standard / FR grade) | ~40% | Stable | Most cost-effective; good impact absorption; flame-retardant grades (UL94 V-0) available |
| PC/ABS blend | ~35% | Growing | Best combination of impact strength + flame retardance; preferred where IK07+ vandal ratings required |
| Flame-retardant polycarbonate (PC) | ~15% | Stable | Higher temperature resistance; transparent-capable variants for LED indicator windows |
| Zinc alloy die-cast | ~10% | Declining | Metal alternative; heavier; corrosion-resistant; rare at this product tier |

**Confidence:** MEDIUM | **Source:** Credence Research PC/ABS Market 2024 ($4.67B); over 58% of electronic enclosures now require UL94 flame-retardant grades (Business Research Insights 2024).

**School-market note:** Schools may require IK07 or IK08 vandal-resistance ratings for devices in student-accessible corridors. PC/ABS blend is the recommended specification for school deployments; standard ABS is acceptable only for staff-only or locked areas.

---

sections.title: B.4 — Component Category Map: Wireless Sensor Module
sections.content:
The wireless radio module appears at one BOM position but faces different competitors depending on the school deployment context.

| Position | BOM path | Function | Safety-critical | Key competitor alternatives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1: Single-door standalone | S3 → L3 RF Transmitter → L2 EasyWave PCB | Alarm event to standalone receiver | No | BLE 5.x (direct phone notification), Z-Wave |
| P2: Multi-door school network | S3 → L3 RF Transmitter → L2 EasyWave PCB | Alarm events aggregated via BUS gateway to BMS | No | LoRaWAN (campus range), Zigbee mesh |
| P3: District-wide platform | S3 → L3 RF Transmitter → L2 EasyWave PCB | Events to cloud SaaS monitoring dashboard | No | LTE-M/NB-IoT (carrier-based, no gateway), cloud-native BLE gateways |

**Insight:** The same hardware slot faces entirely different integration requirements at each deployment scale. A 5-door primary school (P1) tolerates EasyWave. A 200-door district-wide rollout (P3) cannot — IT procurement policies and district BMS standardization override the local alarm functionality requirement.

---

sections.title: Key Findings for Schools Market
sections.content:
| Finding | Implication |
|---|---|
| **Protocol lock-in (C6) is the #1 BOM risk** | EasyWave's ~45% legacy share is declining. School IT departments standardizing on LoRaWAN or BLE-based platforms will reject GfS unless a gateway integration path is documented upfront. |
| **Lithium primary battery is operationally superior** | Gym exits + corridor exits in schools are high-frequency doors. Specifying lithium 9V at time of sale reduces year-2 maintenance callbacks and improves C4 compliance. |
| **PC/ABS or IK-rated housing required for corridors** | ABS is adequate for staff-only areas. Vandal-exposed doors (student corridors, locker room exits) require IK07+ rating. GfS should confirm IK rating or specify PC/ABS variant. |
| **Piezo transducer is the only C2-compliant alarm technology** | Electromagnetic and speaker alternatives are incompatible with the mechanical-alarm-at-0V absolute constraint. No substitution is possible without violating fire code. |
| **School security market growing at 11–13% CAGR** | $3B+ US+EU market in 2024; 48% of US districts upgraded access/alarm systems in the past year. Timing is favorable for retrofit-friendly, battery-operated solution. |

---

rows.Value: **YES** — BOM is of the *product sold into this market* (Türwächter IoT), not of the school. Physical device with full compositional structure.
Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard — IoT variant (Art. 990140)
Exit Control 179 Pre-alarm + Radio (battery-operated, surface-mount, 868 MHz EasyWave)

rows.School-context relevance: Blocks door handle; single-motion release per EN 179. Non-negotiable under C2 (power-independent).
95 dB alert on activation. Mechanical trigger — zero power required. Fire code mandated.
IoT monitoring layer. Key value driver for facility managers. C6 (protocol lock-in) is primary school risk.
9V battery. C4 (battery life) elevated in schools — high door-use frequency in corridors and gym exits.
Surface-mounted housing. Vandal-resistance concern in schools (C5 surface-mount limitation).

rows.School relevance: Audit trail; preferred in newer school access programs

rows.Notes: Cost-effective; good impact absorption; indoor standard
Flame-retardant grades (UL94 V-0); stronger; preferred where IK vandal ratings matter
Premium; high cost; rarely justified at this product level
Dominant. Low power draw; self-contained oscillator; operates without mains.
Coil-based; louder at low frequencies; higher current draw — less compatible with battery-primary constraint C2.
Enables configurable tones/voice alerts; significantly higher power; rare in battery-primary devices.

rows.School fit: Dominant in legacy alarm systems; no native BMS interop; GfS BUS gateway required.
Short range (~30m); native iOS/Android app; no hub required for small deployments. Good for single-school use.
Mesh topology; used in building automation; requires coordinator hub.
Long range (4–6 floors through concrete); growing in campus deployments; requires LoRaWAN gateway. Best fit for large school campuses.
Easy IT integration; high power draw; battery life concern (C4).
Carrier-grade reliability; subscription cost per device; limited to high-security or national chains.
Universally available; low upfront cost; shorter life under high frequency
2–3× life; higher upfront; best choice for hard-to-access doors and gym exits
Reduces waste; operationally intensive; not recommended for unattended locations
Emerging in premium IoT devices; not available in current GfS product line

rows.Notes: Most cost-effective; good impact absorption; flame-retardant grades (UL94 V-0) available
Best combination of impact strength + flame retardance; preferred where IK07+ vandal ratings required
Higher temperature resistance; transparent-capable variants for LED indicator windows
Metal alternative; heavier; corrosion-resistant; rare at this product tier

rows.BOM path: S3 → L3 RF Transmitter → L2 EasyWave PCB
S3 → L3 RF Transmitter → L2 EasyWave PCB
rows.Function: Alarm events aggregated via BUS gateway to BMS

rows.BOM path: S3 → L3 RF Transmitter → L2 EasyWave PCB
rows.Function: Events to cloud SaaS monitoring dashboard
rows.Key competitor alternatives: LTE-M/NB-IoT (carrier-based, no gateway), cloud-native BLE gateways

rows.Implication: EasyWave's ~45% legacy share is declining. School IT departments standardizing on LoRaWAN or BLE-based platforms will reject GfS unless a gateway integration path is documented upfront.
Gym exits + corridor exits in schools are high-frequency doors. Specifying lithium 9V at time of sale reduces year-2 maintenance callbacks and improves C4 compliance.

rows.Finding: **PC/ABS or IK-rated housing required for corridors**
rows.Implication: ABS is adequate for staff-only areas. Vandal-exposed doors (student corridors, locker room exits) require IK07+ rating. GfS should confirm IK rating or specify PC/ABS variant.

rows.Finding: **Piezo transducer is the only C2-compliant alarm technology**
rows.Implication: Electromagnetic and speaker alternatives are incompatible with the mechanical-alarm-at-0V absolute constraint. No substitution is possible without violating fire code.
$3B+ US+EU market in 2024; 48% of US districts upgraded access/alarm systems in the past year. Timing is favorable for retrofit-friendly, battery-operated solution.

market.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)
market.product_l5: GfS Türwächter Exit Control 179/1125 with IoT (Art. 990140)

bom_units.name: Mechanical Guard Subsystem
bom_units.description: Spring-loaded physical guard that blocks door handle depression in locked state; 45° rotation releases handle and triggers alarm via mechanical linkage. Zero electrical power required.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

bom_units.name: Acoustic Alarm Subsystem
bom_units.description: 95 dB audible alarm triggered mechanically on guard rotation. Piezoelectric transducer driven by oscillator circuit. Operates without electrical power (purely mechanical trigger).
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

bom_units.name: Wireless Communication Subsystem
bom_units.description: 868.3 MHz EasyWave radio transmitter broadcasting guard state and alarm events to receivers and BUS interfaces. IoT supervisory layer — does not affect escape route compliance.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

bom_units.name: Power Supply Subsystem
bom_units.description: 9V replaceable block battery powering IoT radio module and pre-alarm circuit. Mechanical alarm requires no power. Battery life target: >2 years at typical door event frequency.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

bom_units.name: Enclosure and Mounting Subsystem
bom_units.description: Injection-molded housing in RAL 6029 safety green; surface-mounted to existing door handle with rear mounting plate. IP45 variant available for outdoor use.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

bom_units.name: Guard Pivot Mechanism
bom_units.description: Spring-return pivot assembly and handle-blocking element forming the mechanical locking action.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

bom_units.name: Key Override Mechanism
bom_units.description: Profile half-cylinder lock allowing authorized bypass without triggering alarm.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

bom_units.name: Sound Generation Module
bom_units.description: Piezoelectric transducer assembly producing 95 dB alarm on guard activation.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

bom_units.name: RF Transmitter Module
bom_units.description: 868 MHz EasyWave radio module transmitting guard-state and alarm events wirelessly to receivers up to 30m.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

bom_units.name: Primary Cell Battery
bom_units.description: 9V block battery (user-replaceable). Chemistry is a design choice with major operational impact for schools.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)
